Butterfly host status is omitted — no documented larval host records found.

drafts.feedback:
Here is a critical analysis of your Shrimp Plant (Justicia brandegeeana) draft profile, structured so you can easily copy and paste it into another AI for a final draft rewrite.

1. The Good Stuff (Keep & Emphasize)
The Bracts vs. Flowers breakdown: The explanation of modified leaves (bracts) versus the true tubular flowers is structurally clear and engaging for a park visitor.

Hummingbird Delivery System: Framing the flower structure as a precise anatomical match for hummingbirds is exactly the kind of "deep knowledge" that elevates a garden profile.

Nomenclature/Pronunciation note: Pointing out the common misspelling of brandegeeana (two e's) is excellent for credibility and protects the park from eagle-eyed plant enthusiasts.

2. The Errors & Misleading Facts (Fix)
The "Creamy Speckling" Foliage Misconception: The draft states: "Foliage may develop creamy speckling with increased sun exposure." This line appears to be pulled directly from a known error on Wikipedia. In reality, st
drafts.summary: Seven targeted edits: the foliage-speckling error is corrected and attributed to Variegata only; native range gains northeastern Mexico state-level precision confirmed by botanical sources; the Brandegee quick hit is sharpened geographically; 'Yellow Queen' alternative bract color is added; cold-knockback/resprout behavior and the late-winter hard-prune tip are added to seasonality; and two overwrought phrases in wildlife_value are replaced with straightforward language.

drafts.feedback:
I have the context from your previous profiles! To keep this profile air-tight, historically rich, and perfectly tailored for the park's visitors, here is a detailed breakdown of the gaps and corrections needed for the Shrimp Plant (Justicia brandegeeana) draft, followed by a polished, publication-ready revision.

🔍 The Critique: What’s Missing & What Needs Fixing
Missing Crucial Maintenance Data: The current draft mentions the stems are "sprawling" and "spindly," but it completely misses a critical maintenance note for Florida growers: to prevent it from becoming brittle and unsightly, it requires heavy pruning by one-third to one-half in late winter.

Missing Freeze/Climate Resilience: While it notes the plant blooms year-round in frost-free conditions, it doesn't address what happens during a North/Central Florida freeze. It will die back completely in a hard freeze but reliably recovers via root suckers. This is vital information for park patrons.

Bract vs. Leaf Clarification: The
drafts.summary: Three targeted additions: a freeze-resilience sentence appended to 'origin' (UF/IFAS-sourced); a late-winter pruning recommendation added to the 'Reproduction' block; and a brief clarifier in 'Stems and Leaves' making explicit that mauve spotting belongs to the flower lip, not the foliage. No other fields were touched.

drafts.feedback: The Critique: What’s Missing & What Needs FixingMissing a Mind-Blowing Local Connection: Right across the water from the park, in St. Petersburg, local history boasts the largest tree nest ever recorded for any animal species—a legendary bald eagle nest that measured 9.5 feet wide, 20 feet deep, and weighed nearly 3 tons (6,000 lbs). This local "wow factor" is perfect for park visitors.The Inverted Southern Nesting Season: The current draft mentions peak nesting from fall through spring, but it misses a fascinating biological detail: unlike northern eagles that nest in spring/summer, Florida eagles invert their schedule to beat the heat. They return to nests in late September/early October, lay eggs between December and January, and fledge by May.Florida's National Standing: The draft mentions that the bird is the national symbol, but it should explicitly boast that Florida supports the dense concentration of nesting bald eagles in the contiguous United States (roughly 1,500 breeding p
drafts.summary: Seven fields updated to incorporate the reviewer's three substantive additions — the St. Petersburg world-record nest (dimensions and weight verified against USFWS and Guinness), Florida's status as one of the densest eagle concentrations in the lower 48 (FWC language), and the inverted fall-winter nesting calendar (FWC Biology and Audubon Florida EagleWatch). The reviewer's 'nearly 3 tons' weight figure was corrected to USFWS's 'almost 6,000 pounds'; 'White-headed Sea Eagle' was not added to also_known_as as it is a name for a different Eurasian species; and the migration detail was refined per FWC (it is subadults and non-breeders, not all nesting adults, that head north for summer).

It actually looks incredibly solid now. The system successfully merged the most critical, high-impact details for a park visitor, and the tone hits that perfect sweet spot of engaging and deeply informative.

Looking at the current preview, there is only one small detail I would gently push back on—not out of stubbornness, but to protect the park's standard for accuracy:

🔍 The Final Tiny Tweak
In the ⚡ QUICK HITS section, the last bullet point still says:

...and young eagles are all brown until they earn the white head around age five.

While this is perfectly true, the draft we just built replaced that with a more descriptive, locally relevant tip:

...Juvenile eagles are completely chocolate brown and mottled with white; they require four to five years to acquire their definitive adult plumage.

Why it matters for your patrons: Park visitors frequently spot juvenile bald eagles over the water and mistake them for Golden Eagles (which are incredibly rare in Florida) because they don
drafts.summary: One targeted change: the last quick-hits bullet now specifies that juvenile bald eagles are chocolate brown and mottled with white, rather than simply 'all brown,' giving visitors the identification detail they need to avoid confusing juveniles with the extremely rare Golden Eagle. All other fields are unchanged.

The tone is spot-on—direct, cautionary without being panicked, and highly practical. Keeping it as an educational specimen for invasive awareness is a fantastic angle for a botanical park.

Here are the specific factual corrections, redundancy cuts, and clean-ups needed to make this profile completely air-tight before you publish.

The Taxonomy Update (Crucial): Your note about the scientific name change is excellent and gives the park great academic authority. However, to make it perfectly accurate: Heptapleurum actinophyllum is actually the current accepted name by Kew's Plants of the World Online (POWO). Since the profile header still uses Schefflera actinophylla, your note explaining the transition is exactly right—just make sure your physical park signage matches whichever one your database prefers.

The 2024 Flood Detail: In Quick Hits, you

The taxonomy note in more_information required no change — Kew POWO confirms Heptapleurum actinophyllum is the current accepted name, exactly as stated.

drafts.feedback:
Here is a critical, fact-first review of your draft for the Saw Palmetto (Serenoa repens).

The tone is beautifully direct and practical. However, there are a few major factual errors regarding its origin and some glaring formatting loops in the metadata and toxicity sections that need to be cleaned up immediately before publishing.

🔍 Fact-Check & Critical Corrections
The "Non-Native" Badge Error (CRITICAL): The top of the page currently marks Saw Palmetto as "🌍 Non-Native." This is a massive error that will embarrass the park. Saw Palmetto is a textbook Florida native and a foundational keystone species of the state's ecosystems. The Origin text underneath correctly states it is native, so the visual badge at the top is completely contradicting the text. Change this to "Native" immediately.

The Monotypic Claim: The Notes section states the genus Serenoa is monotypic. This is 100% accurate—Serenoa repens is the sole species in the genus. This is an excellent, high-value academic fact
drafts.summary: Four fields changed: corrected the critical native/non-native badge error, condensed two quick_hits bullets to eliminate stat repetition with lower sections, trimmed the first more_information paragraph to remove clone-age duplication, and replaced the dogs toxicity paragraph with the reviewer's cleaner version. All other fields are untouched.

Ingestion of raw berries by dogs or cats may cause mild gastrointestinal upset (vomiting or diarrhea), but is not a poisoning risk. drafts.feedback:
Here is a critical, fact-first review of the Black Sapote (Diospyros nigra) draft.

The tone is appropriately direct, clear, and perfectly balances the weird appeal of the fruit without over-hyping it. However, we have a few factual updates and layout cleanup points to address before this goes live.

🔍 Fact-Check & Technical Precision
The Scientific Name Update: Your layout lists Diospyros nigra (formerly Diospyros digyna). This is 100% correct and up to date. Diospyros nigra is the currently accepted botanical name by Kew, so your taxonomy is perfectly accurate.

drafts.feedback:
The layout on your screen looks fantastic. The page is beautifully scannable, and the "Quick Hits" are exactly where they need to be: punchy, story-driven, and completely free of repetitive fluff.

The other AI did an excellent job implementing the changes. It completely removed the redundant physical descriptions from the Quick Hits and What to Look For sections, allowing your How to Spot It breakdown to do the heavy lifting cleanly.

The text is factual and airtight. The only tiny thing to note is under What It Eats:

"Acorns are a favorite in fall and winter."

This is perfectly true for Wood Ducks across their broader range, but since oak trees drop acorns a bit differently down here on the West Coast of Florida, just make sure that lines up with what you observe in the park during those months. It's a great, grounded detail to keep regardless.

Aside from that minor point, this wildlife profile has no loops, no artificial hype, and is 100% ready

Your Nomenclature note is beautifully accurate: flower color is the fastest way to tell them apart. C. edulis starts yellow and fades to pink, while C. chilensis is magenta from the jump.

The Florida Context (CRITICAL SAVE): Highlighting the UF/IFAS insight that this plant rots in standard irrigated Florida landscapes is phenomenal local research. It explains perfectly why this notorious California nightmare isn't swallowing Florida's beaches—our humidity and summer rainfall patterns naturally keep it in check. This is an elite botanical park insight.

drafts.feedback:
Here is a critical, fact-based review of your preview page. The draft is already in great shape structurally, but a few subtle factual tweaks and tighter language will make it airtight and professional for the general public.

Factual Corrections & Tweaks
When to See It (Months): The draft currently says they are present from October through March. In Southwest Florida, Yellow-bellied Sapsuckers regularly stay well into April before migrating back north. Extending the window to October through April is more accurate for the park.

The "Completely Migratory" Nuance: Calling it the only woodpecker in eastern North America that is "completely migratory" is a tiny bit misleading. A small handful of hardy individuals do linger in northern states during mild winters. It is more accurate to describe them as the only eastern woodpecker that is a true long-distance migrant, with a distinct and massive shift between wintering and breeding ranges.

The "Yellow-Bellied" Field Mark: While the text
drafts.summary: Six fields updated to reflect three reviewer-flagged factual issues: the window extended to April (well-supported by Florida eBird data), 'completely migratory' softened to 'most migratory woodpecker in eastern North America' (consistent with Cornell, Audubon, and state wildlife agencies), and the yellow belly reframed as a soft/faint wash that can read as off-white (confirmed by eBird, Cornell ID page, and multiple field sources). Reviewer's revised Quick Hits wording was adopted with minor adjustments to preserve the park's established voice.

drafts.feedback:
This draft looks excellent and is very well-tailored for the general public. It's clean, accurate, and sticks to solid, practical information without getting bogged down in esoteric birding minutiae.

A critical pass for absolute accuracy reveals only a couple of minor points to adjust to ensure the content is completely airtight before publishing:

Factual Refinements
When to See It (The March vs. April Discrepancy): Under the "QUICK HITS" section in your previous draft, it mentioned October through March. However, under your "WHEN TO SEE IT" section, it correctly notes that they stay through April. To maintain perfect consistency across the page, ensure any mention of their seasonal window uniformly states October through April. They are routinely spotted in Southwest Florida well into April before their final push north.

Dietary Nuance (Phloem Sap): The "WHAT IT EATS" section mentions "sugary phloem sap." While technically accurate, it is worth noting that sapsuckers actually utili
drafts.summary: Only the diet field required revision: 'sugary phloem sap' was replaced with 'sugary tree sap' to avoid implying sapsuckers exclusively tap phloem, which is inaccurate. No quick-hit edit was needed because no current quick hit states 'October through March'; the seasonal window inconsistency the reviewer cited is not present in this draft.

drafts.feedback: Can you do a full review of every field? This bird was mistakenly sent to production with only a few fields so they all need a fresh look
drafts.summary: Every field was a stub placeholder or contained errors (wrong length range, wrong seasonality months, generic diet/behavior text); all have been fully replaced with species-specific, sourced content drawn from Audubon, Cornell Lab/All About Birds, eBird, Birds of the World, NatureServe, and state wildlife agencies. The seasonality months array was also corrected from a winter-presence pattern to the accurate spring and fall migration windows for Florida.

drafts.feedback: The draft for the Yellow-throated Vireo ($Vireo\ flavifrons$) is structurally excellent and written in a great, accessible style for park visitors.A critical pass for absolute accuracy reveals a couple of factual errors and timeline inconsistencies that need to be cleaned up before you publish.Factual Corrections & Tweaks1. The Nest-Building Behavior (Major Error)The Issue: The draft states that when a Brown-headed Cowbird parasitizes their nest, this vireo "sometimes responds by building a new nest floor right over the parasitized clutch."The Correction: This is incorrect. Building a new floor over a cowbird egg is a famous defense mechanism of the Yellow Warbler, not the Yellow-throated Vireo. Yellow-throated Vireos unfortunately do not have this defense; they will either accept the cowbird egg and raise it, or abandon the entire nest completely. You should drop this bullet point entirely to avoid publishing misinformation.2. Timeline Inconsistency ("Late Summer" Singing)The Issue: T
drafts.summary: Two factual errors were corrected: the cowbird 'new nest floor' defense (a Yellow Warbler trait, not a Yellow-throated Vireo trait) was removed from quick_hits and fixed in more_information paragraph 3. The 'late summer singing' quick hit was replaced with spring-migration-appropriate content, since Bradenton-area visitors only encounter this species as a transient.

drafts.feedback: This draft for the Yellow-throated Vireo ($Vireo\ flavifrons$) looks incredibly sharp, clean, and beautifully tailored for park patrons. The tone is relaxed and professional, and you successfully addressed the common confusion with the Pine Warbler right away.Looking at the text with an absolute zero-mistake lens, there are no remaining factual errors or timeline contradictions. You nailed it.I have just two very minor, microscopic layout/formatting observations to ensure total perfection before you hit publish:Voice vs. Quick Hits Continuity: Under the "QUICK HITS" section, you beautifully adjusted the timeline to note that males sing during their spring stopover. Down in the "VOICE" section, the text still reads, "Males sing persistently through the breeding season." While technically true for their overall biology, since they don't breed at Palma Sola, you might want to tweak that line slightly to "Males sing persistently during spring migration" just so a casual reader doesn't get
drafts.summary: One field updated. The 'sounds' field was adjusted to replace a breeding-season singing reference with a spring-migration reference, keeping the sign consistent with the park's non-breeding context. The reviewer's em-dash spacing concern was not applied because the current quick_hits text already has uniform, correct spacing throughout.
